what_it_does: Renders a self-contained React design-canvas page that compares four navigator drawer treatments (V1 Index, V2 Tiles, V3 Stepped Flow, V4 Compact Directory) inside a shared two-rail shell (global rail + project-zone rail), using inline CSS and static sample data.
observations: Self-contained HTML loads React 18, ReactDOM and Babel standalone from unpkg with integrity hashes.
Imports an external stylesheet (styles/colors_and_type.css) and two Babel JSX modules (src/icons.jsx, design-canvas.jsx).
Defines a large block of CSS that establishes shared navigator primitives (rails, icon holders, project header, gold active state) and four per-variation layouts.
Hardcodes sample data: PROJECT, GLOBAL rail items, ZONES rail items, a BUILD zone with three pages, and an OPS zone with six pages.
Provides reusable Rails and ProjHeader components used by every variation.
Four drawer components map zone.pages into rows or cards and apply an .on class for the active page.
Uses DesignCanvas, DCSection, DCArtboard and DCPostIt from the external design-canvas.jsx.
Includes a second section that compares V1 and V4 against the denser OPS zone.
References many CSS custom properties (e.g. --paper, --ink, --accent-gold, --ms-hairline) that are defined in the external colors_and_type.css.
Icons are rendered by name through a global window.Icon component.
standouts: A strong shared visual language: 42px row rhythm, rounded-square icon holders, warm-paper backgrounds, gold active treatment, and hairline dividers unify all four directions.
The drawer is intentionally treated as the rail widened, not a separate panel.
Four distinct information architectures are generated from the same page/status data structure.
An explicit density-check artboard tests how the two list directions scale to a six-page zone.
conventions: BEM-ish class prefixes: nv- for shared chrome, v1-/v2-/v3-/v4- for variation-specific styles.
CSS custom properties for theming, with values supplied externally.
React functional components and JSX via Babel in the browser.
Static uppercase constants for data; props pass zone and activeId into drawer components.
External icon and canvas abstractions are assumed to exist.
concerns: Depends on external files (colors_and_type.css, icons.jsx, design-canvas.jsx) that are not shown; it will not render standalone without them.
All state is hard-coded, so there is no real navigation, routing, or selection behavior.
Browser-side Babel compilation is suitable for a prototype but not for production.
Some CSS comments are long and editorial, increasing maintenance surface.
V4 active rows keep their subtitle hidden (max-height:0, opacity:0), which looks like a placeholder or unfinished behavior.
Navigation buttons lack accessibility attributes such as aria-current, aria-expanded, or role attributes.
notes: A design-prototype HTML page that uses React to render four competing navigator drawer treatments against a fixed two-rail chrome, plus a density check on a six-page zone.
questions: What icon set does window.Icon expose, and are all referenced icon names guaranteed to exist?
How is a zone/page selection intended to integrate with real routing or application state?
Does colors_and_type.css define every custom property used here, including status color variants?
Is the 452px artboard width the target drawer width or only a presentation size?
Why does the V4 active state hide the subtitle instead of revealing it?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose is to explore four drawer directions that share the same rail language; the file does exactly that by rendering four artboards and a density check, so purpose and actual match.
apparent_themes: Continuity between icon rails and expanded drawer
Warm paper and gold-accent design system
Shared primitives enabling multiple visual expressions
Scalability and density of navigation patterns
intention_signals: It appears to be trying to converge on a single navigator drawer pattern for a project/zone model by holding the surrounding chrome constant and varying only how the page list is presented, so the team can compare readability, density, and brand expression.
novelty: The systematic side-by-side exploration of four drawer architectures built from one set of CSS primitives and shared rail/project-header components, with an explicit density-check section for a high-page-count zone.
contribution: A reusable two-rail navigator shell (global rail, zone rail, project header) and four drawer layout patterns that show how the same page/status data can be expressed while keeping icon holders, active-state styling, and row rhythm consistent.
summary_for_embedding: Navigator Explorations.html is a self-contained React design-canvas page that renders four alternative project-zone drawer layouts inside a shared two-rail navigator chrome and includes a density check on a six-page Operations zone.
intention_for_embedding: To explore and compare visual treatments for the drawer layer that appears when a project zone is selected, while keeping the global and zone rails unchanged.
